Effects of Cranioplasty on Contralateral Subdural Effusion After Decompressive Craniectomy: A Literature Review
Wu Zhou 1 , Zhihua Wang 1 , Huaxin Zhu 1 , Zhiping Xie 1 , Yeyu Zhao 1 , Chengcai Li 1 , Shenke Xie 1 , Jilai Luo 1 , Meihua Li 1 , Jianguo Yao 2
From the 1 Department of Neurosurgery, The First Affiliated Hospital of Nanchang University, and 2 Jiangxi Academy of Medical Sciences, Nanchang, Jiangxi Province, China
To whom correspondence should be addressed: Meihua Li, M.D., Ph.D.

To whom correspondence should be addressed: Meihua Li, M.D., Ph.D.

Contralateral subdural effusion (CSE) is de /uniFB01 ned as a new subdural effusion (SE) on the opposite side of the decompressive craniectomy (DC) and has the same computed tomography density as cerebrospinal /uniFB02 uid (CSF). 1,2 Cases diagnosed with CSE after decompressive craniectomy (CSEDC) are rarely reported. 3-5 Although many theories have been proposed to explain the development of CSE, the pathogenesis by which it develops remains somewhat obscure. Most SE cases are asymptomatic and

it develops remains somewhat obscure. Most SE cases are asymptomatic and clinically silent. 6 A risk for delayed or missed diagnosis may exist for patients without obvious clinical symptoms or signs. Only patients with deteriorated clinical status

-BACKGROUND: Contralateral subdural effusion (CSE) after decompressive craniectomy (CSEDC) is occasionally observed. Cranioplasty is routinely performed for reconstruction and has recently been associated with improving contralateral subdural effusion. We sought to systematically review all available literature and evaluate the effectiveness of cranioplasty for CSE.

-METHODS: A PubMed, Web of Science, and Google Scholar search was conducted for preferred reporting items following the guidelines of systematic review and meta-analysis, including studies reporting patients who underwent cranioplasty because of CSEDC.

-RESULTS: The search yielded 8 articles. A total of 56 patients ranging in age from 21 to 71 years developed CSEDC. Of them, 32 patients underwent cranioplasty. Eighteen cases with symptomatic CSE underwent cranioplasty alone, 2 cases received Ommaya drainage later because of a recurrence of CDC, and 1 case underwent a ventriculoperitoneal shunt because the CSE did not resolve completely and the ventricle was dilated again. The symptoms of 14 cases lessened without recurrence after simultaneous

The symptoms of 14 cases lessened without recurrence after simultaneous cranioplasty and drainage or a shunt. The total success rate (CSE disappeared without recurrence) was 90.6% for patients who underwent cranioplasty; however, the total incidence of hydrocephalus was 40.1%.

-CONCLUSIONS: This review suggests that cranioplasty is effective for the treatment of CSEDC, particularly intractable cases, but early cranioplasty may be more effective. In addition, hydrocephalus is fairly common after cranioplasty and requires further treatment.
undergo imaging examinations and are eventually diagnosed with CSE. Some researchers have suggested that more aggressive treatments are needed because CSE tends to cause a midline shift and neurologic deterioration. 7

Cranioplasty is performed to reconstruct the skull and potentially prevent some complications. It has long been observed that cranioplasty often facilitates neurologic recovery after traumatic brain injury (TBI) by restoring intracranial pressure (ICP) dynamics and allowing increased rehabilitation activities. 8,9 Published reports describing the effects of cranioplasty on CSE are mostly in the form of case reports and small series

( Figure 1 ). This systematic review evaluated the reported effects of cranioplasty on CSE and the associated changes in neurologic function.
A PubMed, Web of Science, and Google Scholar search adherent to Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses was performed for all articles containing the terms ' cranioplasty ' and ' subdural effusion, ' or ' subdural hygroma. ' No restrictions on the date of publication were made. Articles
Figure 1. PRISMA flowchart illustrating the details of the literature search and review for studies describing the effect of cranioplasty on contralateral subdural effusion.

were limited to English and studies with human subjects. Eligible cases were extracted from the articles (case reports, case series, cohort studies, randomized controlled trials, systematic reviews, and meta-analyses). Two researchers (Wu Zhou and Zhihua Wang) independently reviewed the articles, and discrepancies were solved by consensus with the co/uniFB01 rst authors. The references of the included studies were also reviewed to identify additional relevant studies.

After the initial list of studies was generated, the titles and abstracts were reviewed. Reviewers discussed and ensured consistency in the application of inclusion criteria before selecting a study. Studies were selected if they met the following criteria: 1) reports that contained cases with CSEDC; 2) reports that contained imaging examinations before and after cranioplasty; 3) reports that contained follow-up information after cranioplasty. A full-text review of all target literature was
after cranioplasty. A full-text review of all target literature was conducted following the title and abstract reviews. Studies and cases that did not meet the inclusion criteria were excluded.

The detailed information included age, sex, the reason for DC, imaging examination before and after cranioplasty, results of clinical follow-up and prognosis, and time from DC to CSE. However, not all studies provided detailed information on each case. Therefore the comparative analysis was limited by the nature of the source data. Statistical analysis of this review was not performed because a comparative analysis was not available.

A total of 37 studies were returned after repeated searches, and 8 met the inclusion criteria. A /uniFB02 ow chart of the screening process is illustrated in Figure 1 . Relevant information is summarized in Table 1 .

Of the total of 56 patients (male ¼ 41, female ¼ 15) who developed CSEDC (age 21 e 71 years old), 32 cases underwent cranioplasty ( Tables 1 e 3 ). All cases received DC surgery due to severe TBI or intracranial hypertension resulting from massive cerebral infarction; necessary imaging examination before and after cranioplasty was reported in all studies. The time suffering from CSEDC was also reported and ranged from 1 to 62 days.

As shown in Tables 1 e 3 , 32 patients with symptomatic CSEDC underwent cranioplasty. The ratio of males to females was 3:1 (24/8). The ages of the participants ranged from 21 to 71 years, with an average age of 46 years. The average time interval from DC to the con /uniFB01 rmed diagnosis of CSE was 22.1 days. Some CSE patients may recurred after conservative treatment or drainage and were further treated after cranioplasty with or without shunt, but some patients required additional
cranioplasty with or without shunt, but some patients required additional cerebrospinal /uniFB02 uid shunt.

As shown in Table 2 , 18 cases with symptomatic CSE accepted cranioplasty alone. Eight cases initially underwent trepanation and drainage or received a shunt to evacuate the SE. The operation had successfully drained the effusion of these patients in the end. However, the effusion quickly recurred, and complete resolution was achieved after cranioplasty. Nine cases received cranioplasty directly, and CSE disappeared without recurrence in 7 patients, while 2 cases received Ommaya drainage later

recurrence in 7 patients, while 2 cases received Ommaya drainage later because of recurrence. One case underwent burr-hole drainage and received a subdural-peritoneal (SP) shunt sequentially, which only temporarily improved the symptoms of the patient. Finally, cranioplasty was performed but the CSE did not resolve completely and the ventricle was dilated again. A ventriculoperitoneal (VP) shunt was ultimately implanted, and the CSE disappeared but the ventricle remained dilated. The

Interval, interval time between decompressive craniectomy and contralateral subdural effusion; M, male; F, female; LP, lumboperitoneal shunting; VP, ventriculoperitoneal shunting; SP, subduroperitoneal shunting.
incidence of hydrocephalus after cranioplasty was 38.9% (7/18), so further shunt implantation might be necessary.

Fourteen cases with symptomatic CSE improved after simultaneous cranioplasty and burr hole drainage, an implantation of an SP shunt, a VP shunt, an Ommaya reservoir, or a lumboperitoneal (LP) shunt. Thirteen cases were treated with cranioplasty and a shunt (10 SP, 1 VP and 2 LP), and their effusion resolved completely. One case was treated with burr hole drainage /uniFB01 rst; however, the effusion recurred. Satisfactory results were achieved after simultaneous accomplishment of burr hole

results were achieved after simultaneous accomplishment of burr hole drainage, cranioplasty, and placement of a VP shunt. In short, these 14 cases who underwent cranioplasty plus burr hole drainage or shunt implantation almost completely resolved without recurrence. However, the incidence of hydrocephalus after surgery was 42.9% (6/14).

As shown in Table 4 , the total success rate (CSE disappeared without recurrence) was 90.6%. The success rate of simultaneous cranioplasty and shunt placement was 100%, which was higher than cranioplasty alone (83.3%). However, the total incidence of hydrocephalus was 40.1%; the incidence of hydrocephalus after simultaneous cranioplasty and shunt placement was 42.9%, slightly higher than cranioplasty alone (38.9%).

Extensive DC is indicated for treating elevated ICP or diffuse brain edema due to severe TBI and ischemic or hemorrhagic stroke. 16-18 However, SE is a well-known complication of DC. 7 SE is de /uniFB01 ned as an acute or chronic accumulation of CSF in the subdural space, with an often changing composition. 19 Most SEs are on the same side of the DC. They are often asymptomatic and resolve spontaneously. This situation can mask the clinical manifestations and delay SE diagnosis. However,

can mask the clinical manifestations and delay SE diagnosis. However, patients without SE had signi /uniFB01 cantly better outcomes compared with those who developed SE after DC according to the Glasgow Outcome Scale. In addition, among the multiple factors in meta-analyses, only basal cistern involvement as viewed via computed tomography was associated with the development of SE after DC. 20 Although SE is a common complication after DC, CSEDC is rare and more likely to be symptomatic. 6 Due
after DC, CSEDC is rare and more likely to be symptomatic. 6 Due to the rarity of the data, it remains unclear how to best manage the CSE cases.
Mechanism Underlying Contralateral Subdural Effusion After Decompressive Craniectomy

The natural history of SE following DC remains unclear. The average time from the DC procedure to the onset of CSE was about 22 days. However, the mechanism by which CSE develops is unclear. Several possible mechanisms are responsible for the development of CSEDC. Tearing or disruption of the arachnoid layer may result in formation of a 1-way /uniFB02 ap valve that promotes CSF leakage and accumulation in the subdural space by shear stress during the trauma or surgery. A disturbance in CSF

space by shear stress during the trauma or surgery. A disturbance in CSF circulation, particularly an absorption problem, would also increase the risk for the effusion accumulating through the torn arachnoid layer. 5,21 CSE

Table 3. Cranioplasty for Symptomatic Cases of Contralateral Subdural Effusion After Decompressive Craniectomy
M, male; F, Female; SP, subduroperitoneal shunting; CSE, contralateral subdural effusion; VP , ventriculoperitoneal shunting; NA, not available.

rarely occurs after DC in stroke patients, 22 suggesting the need for initial arachnoid rupture caused by trauma. A rapid reduction in ICP and outward cerebral herniation after decompression may produce a pressure gradient between the 2 hemispheres, which enlarges the contralateral subdural space and accumulates effusion, particularly when a possible rupture exists in the arachnoid layer after initial brain trauma. 4,5,23 In our experience, the more obvious the brain shift after DC, the more

In our experience, the more obvious the brain shift after DC, the more likely the contralateral subdural effusion. In addition, it has been proposed that shrinkage of the ipsilateral brain results from tissue retraction. A rapid decrease in ICP during the DC would also cause a pressure gradient between the 2 hemispheres and a brain shift, which would /uniFB01 nally lead to enlargement of the contralateral subdural space and accumulation of SE. 5,13
Treatment and Prognosis of Contralateral Subdural Effusion

The optimal treatment for SE has not been determined. Conservative treatment is recommended for asymptomatic SE. The majority of SE cases disappear gradually without surgical intervention. However, CSE may need more active treatment because of the tendency to cause a midline shift and/or neurologic deterioration. If the volume of the CSE and ICP gradually increases and causes a shift in the midline, appropriate surgery should be performed as soon as possible. Surgical options for symptomatic SE

be performed as soon as possible. Surgical options for symptomatic SE include Ommaya catheter drainage; cranioplasty; a temporal muscle sticking operation; placement of an SP, VP, or LP shunt; or a combination of these. 1-7,10,12,13,15,24 Burr hole drainage is considered as a simple procedure for SE. However, a craniotomy defect and a possible resistance gradient between hemispheres may cause the CSE to recur, indicating the resistance of burr hole drainage. 6 The advantage of Ommaya catheter

the resistance of burr hole drainage. 6 The advantage of Ommaya catheter drainage compared with traditional burr hole drainage is that a repeated puncture can be performed for continuous drainage. However, recurrence of CSE cannot be avoided. 24 Treating with a craniotomy for temporal muscle sticking is satisfactory, and intractable CSEs can be completely absorbed. However, the disadvantages of this surgery are that it requires at least 1 operation, a longer hospital stay, and higher medical
requires at least 1 operation, a longer hospital stay, and higher medical costs. 24

Cranioplasty has been reported as a new method for treating CSE. Some patients resolve after cranioplasty, but others require an additional shunt or drainage ( Table 2 ). Wan et al 12 suggested that
Table 4. Comparison of Cranioplasty Solely and Simultaneous Cranioplasty and Shunting
SCDS, simultaneous cranioplasty and drainage or shunt.

cranioplasty restores the dicrotic ICP waveform, helping to promote absorption of the CSE. Salunke et al 6 indicated that cranioplasty prevents the formation of CSE while conservative or burr hole drainage does not. The pressure gradient following DC is the main cause of development of CSE; cranioplasty restores the normal physiologic structure and stable state of the skull. Therefore the compliance and pressure-volume curve of brain tissue tend to be normal, thereby eliminating the pathologic

of brain tissue tend to be normal, thereby eliminating the pathologic basis of imbalance in ICP and promoting the reabsorption of /uniFB02 uid accumulation. Salunke et al 6 suggested that cranioplasty is recommended as the cornerstone for treating CSE. Several studies have reported good results with simultaneous cranioplasty and drainage or shunt placement for CSE. 1,2,14,22 Because of the potential failure of a shunt or drainage for CSE, cranioplasty can be performed at the same time, which

or drainage for CSE, cranioplasty can be performed at the same time, which solves the problem of outward protrusion, and the pressure gradient created by the cranioplasty contributes to the effect of the shunt or drainage on the SE. Drainage or shunt placement plus cranioplasty completely resolves SE aggregation and may promote narrowing and adhesion in the subdural and subarachnoid spaces, resulting in deterioration of CSF circulation and hydrocephalus. 14 Shunt placement plus cranioplasty as

CSF circulation and hydrocephalus. 14 Shunt placement plus cranioplasty as the recommended surgical procedure is applied to patients with acutely impaired consciousness or remarked neurologic de /uniFB01 cits caused by extracerebral injury from CSE. 2

However, how does one determine the best time for cranioplasty? The time for cranioplasty after DC has been critically discussed. 11,25 Cranioplasty (3 e 6 months after DC) does not seem to lower the postcranioplasty infection rate or the need

to divert CSF. 25 Waziri indicated that delayed cranioplasty treatment is associated with the development of persistent hydrocephalus, requiring permanent diversion of the CSF. 26 By contrast, an early cranioplasty could effectively improve neurologic recovery, mainly due to the increased cerebral blood /uniFB02 ow after the procedure. 27 Meanwhile, Salunke et al 6 and Wan et al 12 also demonstrated that early cranioplasty prevents the formation of CSE. Beauchamp et al 25 suggested that

prevents the formation of CSE. Beauchamp et al 25 suggested that cranioplasty should be performed whenever an imaging examination shows reduced brain swelling. Therefore cranioplasty after DC as long as the swelling subsides may improve patient ' s neurologic symptoms and avoid some complications including CSE. Nevertheless, it is important to note that after cranioplasty, some patients may develop hydrocephalus after their CSE has resolved and further shunt implantation might be required.

The majority of the literature consists of small case reports and series and thus limiting the description of the types of SE studies, general /uniFB01 ndings related to cranioplasty, and the range of clinical outcomes. Such case reports and series are inherently subject to reporting and selection biases. Although most current literature suggests that cranioplasty is effective for treating CSE, these changes may be related to a variety of factors and the cause cannot be assumed. Meta-analyses

to a variety of factors and the cause cannot be assumed. Meta-analyses are not feasible due to the limited number of eligible studies and their variability. Ultimately, only a qualitative rather than quantitative assessment of these studies can be performed.

CSE may be a common complication after DC; however, most patients are asymptomatic and thus are unacknowledged or the clinical symptoms are masked for some reason, resulting in a missed diagnosis. This review also con /uniFB01 rms that cranioplasty after DC is effective for treating CSE, particularly intractable cases, and early cranioplasty may be more effective. In addition, hydrocephalus is common after cranioplasty and requires further treatment. Although it is widely accepted that

and requires further treatment. Although it is widely accepted that cranioplasty is a treatment for CSE, randomized controlled trials and other prospective cohort studies are necessary to determine the best time for cranioplasty.
